segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a large, enthusiastic audience at a Microsoft Build event, illuminated by vibrant blue and purple stage lighting, many attendees raising their hands or recording the moment, accompanied by the sound of their applause and cheers. The scene transitions to a full-stage view where Satya Nadella, identified by on-screen text as Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, walks confidently across the stage. Dressed casually in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, he greets the audience with an enthusiastic "Good morning, it's fantastic to see you all, welcome to Build!" against a dynamic, colorful abstract backdrop featuring the 'Microsoft Build' logo in bold, modern font with an orange, yellow, and purple gradient. As Nadella speaks, his voice is clear and welcoming, expressing excitement to be back together physically and acknowledging those joining online. He gestures with his hands, occasionally clasping them or using open hand movements to emphasize points, describing developer conferences as "special times, special places to be, especially when platform shifts are in the air." The camera follows him, shifting from wide shots to medium close-ups, capturing his engaged expression and animated delivery as he recalls his first PDC in 1991 and the life-changing impact it had. He conveys an energetic and forward-looking atmosphere, stating it's "exciting to be able to come back to Build 2023 with that same sense of anticipation of something big that is shifting around us as developers," before mentioning reading "Mitchell Waldrop's Dream Machine" last summer. The bright and professional lighting consistently highlights him and the vivid stage design.

segments.integrated_caption: Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, walks confidently across the vibrantly lit stage at the Microsoft Build event, dressed in a dark navy hoodie, gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers. As he gestures expressively, the camera pulls back to reveal a large, engaged Conference Hall Audience, many raising their hands or recording, while massive screens display the 'Microsoft Build' branding. Moving to the center, Nadella introduces a new presentation slide titled 'Pursuit of the Dream Machine,' which features a timeline of computing history. He explains, "while I was playing with DB3 as GPT4 was called then, DaVinci 3... it just brought to it brought in perspective what this is all about. I think that that concept of Dream Machine perhaps best communicates what we have really been doing over the last 70 years." As he walks along the stage, pointing to milestones, he elaborates on the evolution, referencing "Vannevar Bush wrote in his most seminal paper, As We May Think," and "Licklider was the first one who even conceptualized the human computer symbiosis," along with "The Mother of All Demos that came in '68," "the Xerox Alto," and "the PC server one in '91, '93." His deliberate movements and hand gestures, particularly when discussing the 2022 ChatGPT milestone, underscore the dynamic and forward-looking atmosphere, keeping the audience's attention fixed on the evolving visual narrative and his insightful commentary.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, standing confidently on a large stage at the Microsoft Build event, dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles. He speaks in a clear, articulate voice, gesturing expressively as he discusses the evolving 'developer stack' and how 'we build is fundamentally changing,' with the backdrop displaying 'How we build' in bold white letters. The camera then pulls back to reveal a wide shot of the audience in a dimly lit conference hall, illuminated by blue and purple stage lights, with attendees seated in rows. Satya continues, introducing Microsoft’s developer tools: first, 'Codespaces,' which he highlights for enabling environment setup 'in seconds versus minutes,' as a slide shows a GitHub repository interface. He then moves to 'Dev Box,' noting that it can be set up 'in less than an hour' instead of a day, while a Visual Studio Code-like environment is displayed. Next, he presents 'Copilot,' mentioning 'Copilot X' and its role in driving productivity, which is '54% or so up,' as a screen capture demonstrates AI-assisted code generation. Finally, he discusses 'GitHub Actions and Azure Development environments,' emphasizing the desire to 'stay in VS Code' and 'command line' to do 'everything there,' as a slide illustrates deployment workflows. The scene returns to Satya Nadella on stage, now standing beside a large screen displaying a promotional image for a .NET-powered podcast app, with text 'powered by .NET MAUI & Blazor' and download buttons, as he concludes his point about being 'close, close to that dream.' The stage features modern lighting with vertical blue and teal light strips and 'Microsoft Build' in white lettering, reinforcing the professional, tech-focused, and forward-looking atmosphere of innovation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark blue background showcasing four distinct development tools: Codespaces, Microsoft Dev Box, GitHub Copilot, and GitHub Actions and Azure Deployment Environments, each with a screen capture and label. The scene then transitions to Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, a bald man with glasses wearing a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles. He stands on a stage in front of a large screen displaying GitHub Copilot code suggestions, gesturing expressively as he speaks about bringing back "both the joy of programming and the flow of programming," emphasizing that "that ability to be able to stay on task is just so wonderful to see." He continues, stating, "So how we build software is radically different, but what we are going to build as developers is really the story of this developer conference." The camera pulls back to reveal a wide shot of the audience in a dimly lit conference hall, illuminated by blue and purple lights, as Nadella walks confidently across the stage. The screen behind him changes to display the text 'How we build | What we build,' which he gestures toward. He then moves across the stage, pointing to different sections of a dynamic grid of multiple screens showcasing various AI-powered applications and interfaces, including chatbots, code editors, and productivity tools. The grid highlights announcements like 'Microsoft and OpenAI extend partnership' and 'GPT-4 now on Azure OpenAI,' underscoring the integration of AI across Microsoft's ecosystem. Nadella maintains an engaging presence, using hand gestures to emphasize points, while the stage lighting remains focused on him and the vibrant display. He explains that "every week there is something new" and they are "infusing this new AI stack across all layers of it," specifically mentioning starting with "tooling in GitHub" and "Copilot in GitHub," then "Copilot in Power Platform," and "when it comes to productivity, Copilot in Microsoft 365."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, a bald man with glasses dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles. He stands on a stage, gesturing expressively as his clear, confident voice details the pervasive integration of AI across Microsoft's platforms. Behind him, a massive, dynamic digital wall displays a grid of various Microsoft AI and development tools, including GitHub Copilot, Azure OpenAI Service, and ChatGPT interfaces. Nadella speaks of "Copilot in Viva," "business process," "Copilot in Dynamics 365," "industry workflows," "security Copilot," and "Copilot for the web in Bing and Edge," along with "features in LinkedIn which are driven by AI" and the "AI infrastructure, where Azure Open AI APIs and everything else around it," emphasizing that "every layer of the stack is profoundly changing." As the camera pulls back to reveal the full stage, illuminated with blue and green lighting and featuring the 'Microsoft Build' logo on the floor, Nadella announces, "we're going to have 50 plus more announcements." Simultaneously, the background screens transition to display bold text: '50+ new updates.' He then highlights the first major announcement: "we're bringing search grounding in Bing to ChatGPT," as the screen transitions again to 'Announcing Bringing Bing to ChatGPT.' The audience, seated attentively in the darkened conference hall, erupts in enthusiastic clapping and cheering upon Nadella's invitation, "Yeah, you can clap," underscoring the energetic and futuristic atmosphere surrounding Microsoft’s advancements in AI integration.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a wide shot of a dimly lit auditorium, bathed in blue stage lighting, filled with an attentive audience. On a modern stage, featuring vertical teal and blue light panels and a sleek wooden floor, Satya Nadella, Chairman and CEO of Microsoft, confidently presents. Dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, he walks with expressive hand gestures, stating that "ChatGPT is the most sort of fast-growing consumer app we've ever seen." Behind him, a large screen displays 'Bringing Bing to ChatGPT' with a screenshot of the ChatGPT interface, showing 'Browsing with Bing' and 'Code Execution Beta.' He explains that "search grounding is a very key feature" for current and grounded information, expressing excitement for its immediate launch in ChatGPT Plus and upcoming free tier availability, calling it "just the start" of bringing "the best of Bing to the ChatGPT experience" with OpenAI. The screen then dramatically transitions to announce 'Windows Copilot' in bold white text under a pink 'Announcing' tag. Satya declares, "Next, we're bringing the Copilot to the biggest canvas of all, Windows," which is met with enthusiastic applause and cheers from the engaged audience, some raising hands or recording. He adds that "you're going to hear a lot from Panos tomorrow about it," and believes this will "make every user a power user of Windows," before instructing, "Let's roll the video," as the segment concludes with the sound of a video starting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a dynamic, abstract blue and purple animated background, accompanied by a futuristic, ambient synth soundscape that builds in intensity. This transitions into the Windows 11 desktop interface, featuring a vibrant blue, flowing ribbon wallpaper, as an upbeat, modern electronic background music with a driving beat begins to play. The taskbar displays the new 'Search' bar and icons, including the Windows Copilot icon, which is clicked to open the Copilot sidebar. The clean, minimalist Windows Copilot interface appears, offering AI-powered assistance. The user types a query, 'How can I adjust my system to get work done?', and Copilot responds with personalized tips, including enabling a focus timer and switching to Dark Theme. The user selects 'Yes' to both suggestions, triggering the activation of the circular Focus Timer widget and the Dark Theme, which changes the desktop to a darker color scheme. The screen then shows multiple open applications, including Visual Studio Code, a dashboard, and system performance widgets, all now in dark mode, emphasizing productivity, while the electronic music continues. The user then asks Copilot to summarize a document titled 'Contoso Business Plan,' and Copilot generates a concise summary. Next, the user asks for music recommendations, and Copilot suggests Spotify playlists like 'Chill Vibes,' 'Brain Food,' and 'Deep Study.' The user selects 'Chill Vibes,' which opens the Spotify Interface, and the background music continues, consistent with music playing. The user then asks how to create a logo for a coffee business, and Copilot suggests using Adobe Express, launching the app and guiding the user to create a logo with a coffee cup design. The final scene shows the completed logo in the Adobe Express Interface, with Copilot confirming the action and offering further assistance, highlighting seamless integration and workflow simplification across apps, all set to the continuous electronic background music.

segments.integrated_caption: Yusuf Mehdi, CVP, Consumer CMO at Microsoft, stands on a stage with a vibrant, abstract gradient background of orange, pink, and purple hues, dressed in a black zip-up hoodie and dark pants with a white smartwatch. He gestures expressively, holding a small black device, as he excitedly announces, in a clear voice, that they are bringing "chat GPT and Bing together as with the default search experience. To give you higher quality answers and more timely answers." The scene transitions to a wide shot, revealing a large audience, many recording, while a massive screen behind him displays the OpenAI logo and the Microsoft Bing logo connected by a heart symbol, indicating their partnership. The screen then shifts to a live demonstration of the ChatGPT interface, where Yusuf selects 'GPT-4' and enables 'Browsing with Bing Beta.' He types the query, 'What can I expect from the .NET team at Build this week?' As the AI browses the web, Yusuf explains that "the results now are more up to date. They include fresh content, and they include citations," noting that links can be clicked to go straight to Bing-sourced webpages. The interface generates a detailed response listing six highlights from Microsoft Build 2023, including .NET Multi-platform App UI, cloud-native development, performance, C# 12, and .NET 8. The screen then switches to the 'Plugins' mode, initially showing 'No plugins enabled.' Yusuf returns to the stage, continuing his presentation with animated gestures, stating, "We're also excited to announce that we're going to bring interoperability between ChatGPT and Bing for plugins," which is met with audience applause. He adds, "So you write them once and they're going to run everywhere." The video concludes with a close-up of the ChatGPT interface, now showing the 'Plugins' dropdown with 'Instacart' and 'Zillow' enabled, and a new chat window titled 'Welcome to the new Bing,' featuring a search bar and conversation style options like 'More Creative,' 'More Balanced,' and 'More Precise,' as Yusuf mentions having "Zillow and Instacart enabled."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of the clean, minimalist Microsoft Bing interface, where a user queries Chicago neighborhoods with good restaurants. Yusuf Mehdi, CVP, Consumer CMO at Microsoft, is heard explaining that Bing Chat now has the same plugins as ChatGPT, demonstrating how he can search for houses in Chicago, ask for criteria, and learn about neighborhoods. Visually, Bing responds with a list including Lincoln Park and Hyde Park, then retrieves real estate listings from Zillow for Hyde Park under $1 million, displaying property details and images, aligning with Yusuf's audio description of automatically calling Zillow for houses in a specific price range and getting "great options" along with "helpful city guides and maps." The scene transitions to Yusuf Mehdi on stage, gesturing animatedly against a vibrant, abstract background, as he continues to elaborate on how plugins will work "across the entire web" via the Edge browser. The screen then displays a Peach Melba Cake recipe from Fourth Coffee, and Yusuf's voice explains being on a webpage, checking out a recipe, and asking Bing Chat to "give me the ingredients from this webpage." Bing is shown generating a shopping list, excluding items the user already has, as Yusuf highlights Bing's ability to "read the context of the webpage" and "understand those ingredients." The segment briefly returns to Yusuf presenting to an engaged audience in a large conference hall, with the recipe on a large screen beside him, before concluding with the Bing interface refining the shopping list based on user input.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a close-up of the Microsoft Bing interface, showcasing its AI-powered chat feature with a clean, minimalist design in blue and white. The user types a query about neighborhoods in Chicago with good restaurants within walking distance, and Bing responds with a list including Lincoln Park, Hyde Park, Wicker Park, and Gold Coast, each with brief descriptions. The user then asks for houses for sale in Hyde Park under $1 million, prompting Bing to retrieve real estate listings from Zillow, displaying property details and images. The scene transitions to a split-screen view with Yusuf Mehdi, identified as CVP, Consumer CMO at Microsoft, speaking on stage against a vibrant, abstract background with gradient colors of orange, yellow, and purple. He gestures animatedly while explaining the capabilities of the AI-powered Bing. The screen then shifts to a recipe for Peach Melba Cake from Fourth Coffee, with detailed ingredients and a photo of the cake. The user asks for a shopping list, and Bing generates a list excluding salt and flour, which the user already has. The stage view returns, showing Yusuf Mehdi continuing his presentation to an audience in a large conference hall, with the recipe displayed on a large screen beside him. The audience is engaged, with some attendees raising their hands or recording the moment. The segment concludes with a close-up of the Bing interface, where the user requests a shopping list for the remaining ingredients, and Bing provides a refined list, demonstrating its ability to adapt to user input and context.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Microsoft Bing interface, where a user is interacting with a recipe for a Peach Melba Cake. A male speaker's confident voice explains how the AI will "automatically call the Instacart plugin, take those ingredients right off the page and put them into an Instacart shopping," allowing the user to get items delivered with "one click," highlighting this as an "incredible productivity benefit." The scene transitions to a full-screen view of the Instacart website, showing the shopping cart with the added items and a 'Continue Shopping' button, visually confirming the speaker's description. The video then cuts to a wide shot of a large conference hall during a Microsoft Build event, with a speaker, identified as Yusuf Mehdi, on stage in front of a vibrant 'Microsoft 365 Copilot' background. The audience erupts in applause and cheers as the speaker says, "Yeah," before continuing, "Let's show you how you'll be more productive at work." The camera zooms in on Yusuf Mehdi, dressed in a black zip-up hoodie, gesturing as he announces, "I'm going to use Microsoft 365 Copilot." The scene shifts to a screen recording of a Word document titled 'Relecloud Sales Agreement', where Copilot is being used to draft and modify legal clauses. Yusuf Mehdi explains he's in Microsoft Word, needing "some help for drafting a legal contract" with "California law," and will "call three plugins from Thompson Reuters." He then demonstrates Copilot's ability to "understand how to edit the limitation of liability using the Practical Law plugin," which will "read the document, find the paragraph and make that change." The Copilot sidebar suggests replacing a liability clause, which the user accepts, and then provides related legal resources, with Yusuf Mehdi concluding by asking if the user wants to know if the clause is "enforceable under California law," showcasing the AI's real-time legal assistance.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a Microsoft Word document, 'Relecloud Sales Agreement,' displayed in a browser, showing legal text. A Copilot sidebar offers AI-driven suggestions for clause replacement and legal research, referencing Practical Law and Westlaw. The speaker, Yusuf Mehdi, explains that Copilot can call the Westlaw plugin for legal analysis and provide a summary of all changes through document intelligence, presenting them in a simple, easy-to-read table. He emphasizes how Microsoft 365 Copilot in Word, supported by powerful plugins like Thomson Reuters, enables drafting legal contracts in a more powerful way. The scene transitions to a split-screen, with Yusuf Mehdi, identified as CVP, Consumer CMO at Microsoft, speaking animatedly on stage against a vibrant background, while the right side continues to show the Word document with the Copilot sidebar. The video then shifts to a Microsoft Teams chat interface, where Copilot actively responds to a query about 'Contoso's website change management procedures,' listing five steps and providing references. Yusuf Mehdi explains he is engaging with Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams chat to track website changes, noting that Copilot calls the Atlassian plugin for help. As the user types a command to 'Create a Jira ticket to track changes outlined above, due by maintenance date,' a Jira Cloud pop-up appears, which Yusuf Mehdi clarifies specializes in project and issue tracking, automatically pulling the Jira ticket and allowing an owner to be assigned using Azure Active Directory. The user selects an assignee, sets a due date, and clicks 'Create,' successfully creating the Jira ticket, which is confirmed in the Teams chat. The final scene returns to Yusuf Mehdi on stage, continuing his presentation with open hand gestures, highlighting how plugins and Microsoft 365 Copilot enable intelligent reading.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Yusuf Mehdi, CVP, Consumer CMO at Microsoft, standing on stage against a vibrant, abstract gradient background, dressed in a black zip-up hoodie. He speaks confidently, using expressive hand gestures, explaining how Microsoft's tools integrate across business apps and the Microsoft Graph to maintain user flow. He then enthusiastically announces, "Finally, as Satya shared, we're excited to announce the Windows Copilot. I think it's going to change how you use your PC forever. Let me show it to you." The scene transitions to a split-screen, showing Yusuf on the left and the 'Windows Copilot' logo—a blue hexagonal icon with white text—on the right, against a dynamic pink, orange, and purple gradient. Yusuf continues, stating he's in a coding project and wants to configure his PC for creativity and productivity. The video then cuts to a screen recording of a Windows 11 desktop interface, featuring a blue, flowing ribbon wallpaper and Visual Studio Code open with JavaScript code. Yusuf narrates, "All I have to do now is invoke the Windows Copilot," as the user clicks the Copilot icon on the taskbar. The Windows Copilot sidebar, a clean, minimalist blue and white interface, appears on the right, offering AI-powered assistance. Yusuf explains this side pane will be consistent across every app and, "just like with Bing chat, I can now ask it questions like, 'How can I adjust my system to get work done?'" The user types this query, and Copilot responds with tips, including enabling a Focus Session and switching to Dark Theme. Yusuf highlights the ability to "with one click, take action on those suggestions," demonstrating by selecting 'Yes' for both options. This triggers the Focus Timer widget, a circular blue and white countdown, to appear, and the desktop theme instantly transitions to a dark mode, including Visual Studio Code and the taskbar. Yusuf remarks, "I also know that as developers, we like dark mode... So with one click, I'm now here on the dark side." The Copilot sidebar confirms the actions, "Started a Focus Session" and "Switched to Dark Theme," as the video concludes with the desktop in its new dark mode, the Focus Timer active, and Copilot ready for further interaction.

Another male voice then thanks Yusuf, adding, "we have, as Yusuf said, fantastic momentum already building, and this is about really creating that opportunity for developers to reach all users across all of these services."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Satya Nadella, a bald man with glasses, dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, walking confidently across the stage at the Microsoft Build event. He gestures expressively as he speaks, his voice clear and enthusiastic, expressing excitement for the audience to exploit opportunities in the coming months. Behind him, a large screen initially displays a slide titled 'New and existing plugin developers,' showcasing a grid of company logos including Spotify, Oracle, and Trello, indicating a collaborative ecosystem. As Satya continues, stating that the "next thing for us, which is really exciting, is AI Studio," the scene transitions to a new slide announcing 'Azure AI Studio.' He elaborates that this is "the full life cycle tool chain" for building intelligent AI apps and co-pilots, detailing its features: building and training models, grounding Azure OpenAI and OSS models with user data, built-in vector indexing in Azure Search, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) support, creating prompt workflows with prompt flow and orchestration, and built-in AI safety. The stage is illuminated with blue and teal lighting, and the 'Microsoft Build' branding is visible on the floor, reinforcing the event's identity, while the camera alternates between wide shots of the stage and closer views of Satya Nadella as he dynamically presents.

segments.integrated_caption: Satya Nadella, a bald man with glasses, dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, confidently strides across a large stage bathed in blue and teal lighting, using expressive hand gestures as he articulately speaks about the critical importance of "AI safety." He emphasizes that Microsoft has been "hard at work" for the last five years to "build into the tool chain AI safety," integrating principles and processes into their engineering stack. Behind him, a massive screen displays the presentation title 'Announcing Azure AI Safety,' framed by vertical light bars, highlighting three key areas: 'Testing,' 'Provenance,' and 'Deployment.' Satya explains that the real challenge is to embed AI safety into the "everyday tool chain," which they are addressing with "AI Studio." He elaborates on 'Testing,' mentioning the 'Responsible AI dashboard' to ensure development is safe, and the 'Grounding in prompt flow' as a key feature for grounding models. He then moves to 'Provenance,' detailing support for "media provenance" in images and videos, and "watermarking for your neural voice," directly aligning with the screen's points on 'Media provenance in Bing and Designer' and 'Audio watermarking in Custom Neural Voice.' The camera alternates between close-ups of Satya and wide shots of the attentive audience, seated in rows at what appears to be a Microsoft Build event, with some recording or raising hands, absorbing his tech-focused presentation.

Behind him, a massive screen displays a presentation titled 'Announcing Azure AI Safety,' divided into three main sections: 'Testing,' 'Provenance,' and 'Deployment.' Under 'Testing,' the screen lists 'Responsible AI dashboard (text and image support)' and 'Grounding in prompt flow.' Under 'Provenance,' it highlights 'Media provenance in Bing and Designer' and 'Audio watermarking in Custom Neural Voice.' Under 'Deployment,' it shows 'Azure AI Content Safety' and 'Model monitoring (track model performance).' The stage is illuminated with blue and teal lighting, and vertical light bars frame the central screen. The atmosphere is professional and tech-focused, with the audience occasionally raising hands or recording the event. The presentation appears to be part of a larger Microsoft Build event, as suggested by the stage design and audience engagement.

segments.integrated_caption: Satya Nadella, bald and wearing glasses, a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers, stands on a blue and teal-lit stage before an audience, gesturing expressively as he speaks. A large screen behind him displays 'Announcing Azure AI Safety,' detailing features like the Responsible AI dashboard, media provenance in Bing and Designer, and Azure AI Content Safety under the three pillars of Testing, Provenance, and Deployment. Nadella articulately explains the critical importance of making AI applications safe at deployment time, emphasizing continuous monitoring for model drift, and expresses excitement for Azure AI Studio in helping developers build AI with safety first. As he concludes by saying, "Let's roll the video," the scene transitions to a screen capture of the Azure AI Studio interface. A narrator introduces it as a full lifecycle tool to build, customize, train, evaluate, and responsibly deploy the latest next-generation models. The clean, modern interface, with its white background and blue accents, showcases a dashboard with recent jobs, compute instances, and options like 'Create notebook' and 'Add compute,' concluding with a close-up of the 'Chat playground' section, demonstrating practical AI interaction.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a screen capture of the Azure AI Studio's 'Chat playground', where a user is adding a data source, selecting 'Azure Cognitive Search' from options like 'Azure Blob Storage' and 'Upload files'. The narrator explains that "with just a few clicks, developers can ground AI models with their structured and unstructured data to quickly and easily build customized, cutting-edge conversational experiences for their customers." The interface then transitions to 'Data Management', showing settings like 'Limit responses to your data content' and acknowledging usage costs for semantic search. The user tests the assistant by querying "Can you give me some information about your hiking jackets?", and the assistant responds with details about two jackets. The scene shifts to the 'Model catalog' in Azure AI Machine Learning Studio, showcasing models like 'gpt-4-32k' and 'gpt-35-turbo', with the narrator stating that "developers can take advantage of a new model catalog that works with the popular models organizations use, including those from Azure Open AI Service, Hugging Face, and many other open source models," as the user navigates categories like 'Open Source Models' and 'Azure OpenAI Service'. Next, a 'Prompt Flow' interface appears, displaying a flow diagram with nodes such as 'question_embedding' and 'llm_response', where the user edits a prompt for a customer query, and the narrator details that "with Prompt Flow, developers can combine relevant data from your organization and create a detailed prompt to get better results," adding that "Prompt Flow works with foundation, internally developed or open source models, and uses popular open source tools, Langchain and Semantic Kernel." The flow's output, a response about hiking jackets, is then shown. The video then moves to the 'Azure AI Content Safety Studio', where the narrator explains, "with Azure AI Content Safety, we are making it easier for you to test and evaluate your AI deployments for safety," as the user tests a sample text: "Now that I have my Summit Breeze jacket. I'm looking for an axe to cut a path in the forest. Can you recommend one?", adjusting severity thresholds for categories like Violence and Self-harm, with the content being rejected due to Violence. The video concludes with a blue screen displaying "Azure AI Studio" and "to build the next generation of AI applications," reinforced by the narrator's final statement: "This is Azure AI Studio. The trusted tools you need to build the next generation of AI applications."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant 'Microsoft Build' title screen, featuring a gradient of orange, yellow, and purple. The scene then transitions to a large, dimly lit conference hall, where a stage is illuminated with dynamic blue and teal lighting. The Stage Presenter, Satya Nadella, a bald man with glasses in a dark navy hoodie, confidently walks across the stage, gesturing expressively. He is heard saying "AI applications" before a wave of enthusiastic applause and cheering erupts from the Conference Hall Audience, many of whom are raising their hands or recording the moment. As the applause subsides, Nadella continues, stating, "And of course, all AI application start with data." The large screens behind him initially display 'Azure AI Safety' before transitioning to 'Microsoft Fabric,' which Nadella excitedly announces, "And we are really thrilled to be announcing Microsoft Fabric." He describes it as "Data analytics for the era of AI," elaborating that it's a product they've worked on for "multiple years" and "perhaps the biggest launch of a data product from Microsoft since the launch of SQL Server." He details its four key pillars: unifying "compute and storage," "all of the full analytic stack product experiences," "governance with analytics," and "the business model across all the different type of analytics work." The 'Microsoft Build' logo is prominently featured on the stage floor, reinforcing the event's professional and energetic atmosphere focused on innovation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Satya Nadella, a bald man with glasses, dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, actively presenting on a dimly lit stage. Behind him, a dark blue backdrop displays 'Data analytics for the era of AI' and four key pillars: Unified storage and compute, Unified experience, Unified governance, and Unified business model. Satya speaks in a clear, articulate voice, gesturing expressively as he explains how users can leverage the same compute infrastructure for various tasks like SQL or machine learning, stating that this unification will fuel the next generation of AI applications before instructing, "Let's roll the video." The scene then transitions to a clean, light gray interface introducing 'Microsoft Fabric' with a dynamic teal logo and the tagline 'Explore the experience,' showcasing six core components: Data Factory, Synapse Data Engineering, Synapse Data Warehouse, Synapse Data Science, Synapse Real-Time Analytics, and Power BI. A new narrator introduces "Microsoft Fabric, a unified data analytics platform. One product, one experience, one architecture, one business model." The 'OneLake data hub' interface is then shown, highlighting 'Unified data' with datasets from Finance, HR, and Sales domains, including details like ownership, refresh times, and sensitivity levels, as the narrator explains that "Unified data is stored in OneLake, a SaaS data lake for the entire organization." The narrator continues, describing how "Data is integrated and stored in an open format, allowing one copy to be used to train machine learning models, visualize data, and run SQL queries on the lake and data warehouse," which is visually demonstrated by a data science notebook with Python code generating histograms for features like MedInc, HouseAge, and AveRooms, followed by a SQL query editor displaying revenue data. A 'Sales Team' workspace listing various data assets is briefly shown. The video then presents a unified experience interface with a dropdown menu showing integration between Power BI, Data Factory, Data Activator, Synapse, Data Science, Data Warehouse, and Real-Time Analytics, with the narrator stating, "A unified experience brings together all the tools data professionals need: pipelines for orchestrating data movement, experiments for training machine learning models, semantic models for defining key metrics, and much more." This is accompanied by a data pipeline visualization with nodes for 'Copy data' and 'Stored procedure,' and an MLflow notebook with code for tracking experiments. Finally, a Power BI dashboard with customer profile analytics, including demographics, return rates, and reservation types, is displayed before transitioning to the Power BI home screen with recommended reports and recent activity, as the narrator begins to address "And for business users, Fabric..." emphasizing a seamless, integrated data analytics environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Satya Nadella, a bald man with glasses, wearing a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles. He stands on a dimly lit stage, gesturing expressively as he speaks with a clear, articulate voice, emphasizing the need to be on a 'fast pace to build things that help us build this next generation of applications, but build them with safety first.' Behind him, a large screen displays 'Data analytics for the era of AI' and the four pillars of Microsoft Fabric: 'Unified storage and compute,' 'Unified experience,' 'Unified governance,' and 'Unified business model.' The camera pulls back to reveal the modern stage with blue and teal vertical light strips and a large audience. As Nadella continues, the screen transitions to 'What we build' and then 'Why we build,' prompting him to ask the audience, 'one of the things that I think we should ask ourselves as developers, is why do we build? Why do we build technology?' The scene then shifts to a detailed graph titled 'Pursuit of GDP growth,' which Nadella refers to as 'pretty stunning.' This graph, sourced from Maddison Project and OurWorldInData.org, illustrates global GDP on a logarithmic scale from 1000 to today, marking technological revolutions like the printing press, steam engines, and the information revolution. Nadella explains how for 'most of human history,' there wasn't much economic growth or technology, until 'something happened 250 years ago,' referring to the long period from 'the Enlightenment to scientific revolution to the Industrial Revolution,' which he notes was close to 400 years in building. The graph highlights increasing GDP growth multipliers per century, with a projected '+AI' surge, underscoring the professional and forward-looking atmosphere emphasizing AI's transformative economic impact.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Satya Nadella, the Stage Presenter, standing on a dimly lit stage wearing a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles. He gestures expressively as he speaks, with a large screen behind him displaying the text 'Data analytics for the era of AI' and the four pillars of Microsoft Fabric: 'Unified storage and compute,' 'Unified experience,' 'Unified governance,' and 'Unified business model.' The camera then pulls back to reveal the full stage setup, showcasing a modern, sleek design with vertical light strips in blue and teal hues, and a large audience seated in front. The screen transitions to a new slide titled 'What we build' and then 'Why we build,' as Nadella continues his presentation. The scene shifts to a detailed graph titled 'Pursuit of GDP growth,' showing global GDP in trillions of 2011 GKS on a logarithmic scale from 1000 to today, with key technological revolutions marked along the timeline, including the printing press, steam engines, telegraph, electrification, mass production, internal combustion engine, flight, synthetic fertilizer, and the information revolution. The graph highlights the GDP growth multiplier per century, increasing from 0.4x in 1000–1100 to 20x in the current era, with a projected '+AI' surge. The source is cited as Maddison Project, OurWorldInData.org. The atmosphere is professional and forward-looking, emphasizing the transformative impact of AI on global economic growth.

He speaks with a clear, articulate voice, discussing the importance of building next-generation applications with 'safety first,' and later posing the fundamental question 'why do we build technology?' before delving into the historical relationship between economic progress and technology, describing the 'Pursuit of GDP growth' graph as 'pretty stunning' and highlighting key historical periods like the Enlightenment and Industrial Revolution.
audio_updated_entities.Pursuit of GDP growth: A large presentation slide displayed on stage screens, featuring a chronological timeline of global GDP growth from 1000 to today, marked with key technological revolutions and GDP growth multipliers per century, with a projected '+AI' surge in the current era. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a static presentation slide titled 'Pursuit of GDP growth,' displaying a logarithmic graph of global GDP from 1000 to today, marked with key technological revolutions and projected '+AI' surge, alongside GDP growth multipliers. Satya Nadella, dressed in a dark navy hoodie over a gray shirt, black pants, and black sneakers with white soles, walks confidently onto the Microsoft Build stage, gesturing towards the screen as he speaks about 'real progress,' noting the 'slope going upwards' and how information technology has played a role in 'seminal moments' on the march towards that 'dream machine.' He emphasizes that 'we now enter the age of AI and we get to define what that slope looks like going forward for economic growth.' The slide then transitions to a 'Historical index of human development' graph, showing two lines tracking progress from 1870, with a magenta line representing achievement in health, knowledge, and living standards in the United States. Nadella continues, asserting that 'it's not even just economic growth on its own,' but that 'we want economic growth so that we can have human development index growth.' He passionately states, 'We want the lifespans to go up, we want education and prosperity and standard of living to go up everywhere. That's why we build, that's why we innovate, that's why technology exists. It's not for technology's sake, but it is for that broad impact.' As he speaks with expressive hand movements, the camera zooms in before shifting to a dynamic collage of diverse, monochrome images depicting people working and collaborating. This collage then transforms into a vibrant, full-color version, with Nadella still presenting in front of it, the stage illuminated with blue and teal lighting, and the 'Microsoft Build' logo visible on the floor. He then transitions to a personal anecdote, mentioning, 'And to me, this all came together, you know, in January when I was visiting India.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a rural Indian village, showcasing a small shop with hanging snacks and a woman in a yellow and red sari interacting with a vendor, while text overlays state, 'There are many government schemes available to us, but we often don't know about them.' A young man in a white kurta and cap, standing in a dusty village courtyard, speaks directly to the camera in Hindi, explaining that while government schemes exist, they often don't reach people in rural areas. A wide shot reveals the village landscape with a mosque and distant hills before transitioning to a modern office setting. Here, Aaditeshwar Seth, identified as Co-Founder of Gram Vaani and Professor at IIT Delhi, speaks about communities living in 'media dark areas' where traditional media penetration is sparse and literacy challenges limit access to newspapers. The narrative shifts back to the village, showing an elderly man walking down a narrow lane, followed by a group of women and children gathered near a brick wall. Children in school uniforms walk through a village doorway, leading to a classroom where students in colorful headscarves read textbooks. The scene returns to the office, where Smita Gupta, a Lawyer and Technologist at OpenNyAI, speaks in front of a whiteboard, highlighting India's 22 constitutionally recognized languages and over 120 other languages, emphasizing the challenge and opportunity of providing justice in one's own language. A close-up shows code on a screen, followed by Abhigyan Raman, Senior AI Researcher at AI4Bharat, discussing with colleagues. A woman wearing headphones verifies a recording on a tablet with Hindi text displayed on the screen. Abhigyan Raman then explains a system architecture on a digital whiteboard, illustrating 'Govt: Scheme Details,' 'Chat layer (WhatsApp),' 'AI4Bharat Models,' and 'Azure Machine Learning,' while interacting with a colleague. He passionately describes Jugalbandi as a chatbot built to solve linguistic and technological divides, mentioning the collection of millions of parallel sentences for machine translation and thousands of hours of data for speech recognition, and the use of state-of-the-art models like Parshini speech recognition and Azure. The segment ends with a close-up of Abhigyan Raman speaking passionately, emphasizing the technical and social integration of AI in rural development.
